Swiss Re reports 85% rise in 9-month profit, helped by low disaster claims
data.description:
FRANKFURT (Reuters) -Reinsurance company Swiss Re said on Friday that net profit for the first nine months of 2025 rose 85%, helped by low claims from natural disasters.

The net profit of $4.037 billion in the period compares with a profit of $2.182 billion a year earlier.

(Reporting by Tom Sims, Editing by Friederike Heine)

Swiss machine maker feels heat from auto sector slowdown, Trump's tariffs
data.description:
UTZENSTORF, Switzerland (Reuters) -Norbert Steuer, a logistics worker at Swiss precision machinery maker K.R. Pfiffner, has been at the firm for over three decades. Now he is one of 80 out of 105 staff due to be let go as it reels from U.S. tariffs and a struggling auto industry.

The firm, which makes million-dollar machinery used by carmakers including Mercedes-Benz and suppliers such as Robert Bosch, is at the sharp end of a tough European car market that has been buffeted by U.S. President Donald Trump's trade tariffs and a slowdown in key export market China.

The sector slump has hit carmakers and industrial firms hard in manufacturing strongholds like Germany, where many of Pfiffner's European customers are. Part of Taiwanese-owned FFG, Pfiffner was already suffering before Trump announced the tariffs in August, which dashed the firm's U.S. orders.

"It was like a bomb went off," Steuer, 59, said of Pfiffner's job cuts announcement, speaking at the company's base in Utzenstorf, a village in central western Switzerland. 

Steuer, who is scheduled to be laid off next summer, worries about his future. "There's always talk about workers being sought, but are they going to want to hire a 59-year-old?"

'IT'S SOMETHING WE'VE NOT SEEN IN OUR LIFETIMES'

Pfiffner gives an insight into the challenges facing firms down Europe's industrial supply chain and beyond.

Switzerland's jobless rate is low at 3%, but has crept up from under 2% in early 2023. Its mechanical and engineering sector could lose around 30,000 jobs by the end of 2026 if U.S. tariffs were to remain in place, industry group Swissmem says.

Customers like Robert Bosch have announced major layoffs - 13,000, with others, including Mercedes-Benz and tyre maker Continental also cutting headcounts.

In the year through June, German industrial companies shed over 114,000 jobs, more than four times the amount in the previous 12 months, according to a study from consultancy EY. A year prior to that, they created over 65,000 jobs.

"It's something we've not seen in our lifetimes," said Volker Treier, foreign trade chief of the German Chambers of Industry and Commerce.

US TARIFF HIT 'KILLS ANY BUSINESS'

Switzerland has been grappling with 39% U.S. tariffs which have dented its export sector. Still, on Thursday, the U.S. and Switzerland moved close to a deal that could lower the tariffs, potentially saving some jobs.

For Pfiffner, Trump's tariffs dealt a hammer blow to its U.S. order pipeline.

"That kills any business," said Pfiffner CEO Andreas Ewald, adding that with a weaker dollar it had made the firm's exports around 50% more expensive for U.S. buyers.

Around Europe, there are wider signs of pressure on employment, with Britain's labour market cooling in the third quarter. European companies have seen revenues flatline with cost-cutting increasingly needed to burnish profits.

In the U.S., meanwhile, layoffs in October were the highest for the month in over 20 years.

'A DOWNWARD SPIRAL RIGHT NOW, EVERYWHERE'

On Pfiffner's vast factory floor in Utzenstorf, staffing is thin as work dwindles.

Pfiffner's parent group FFG has begun shifting some technology and production to its U.S. plants, said CEO Ewald.

The talk of job cuts is also creating a negative feedback loop for the local economy, said company veteran Steuer.

"There's really a downward spiral right now everywhere, and what do people do then?" he said. "They save money instead of buying a new car, a new TV or a new phone. And on it goes."

(Reporting by Dave Graham; Additional reporting by Mark John; Editing by Adam Jourdan and Emelia Sithole-Matarise)

By Dave Graham

Aviation Lobby: Relief Measures Boost Industry Competitiveness
data.description:
The aviation sector has welcomed the coalition committee's agreements on relief measures for air transport, viewing them as a stimulus for Germany as a business location.

"The federal government has kept its word and put an end to the years-long upward spiral of taxes and fees for air travel from Germany," said Joachim Lang, CEO of the German Aviation Industry Association (BDL), on Thursday evening. "This is an important signal." The planned relief measures are expected to reduce state-imposed location costs for air travel by around ten percent. "This is a good first step toward strengthening the competitiveness of our aviation industry--with positive effects reaching far beyond our own sector."

Lufthansa also described the measures as an important step, stating, "Flying in and out of Germany has become too expensive." In addition to the now-approved reduction in the air traffic tax, it is particularly important to swiftly implement measures to lower fees, a spokesperson for the airline explained. With these decisions, the federal government has shown that it recognizes the seriousness of the situation in the aviation sector. "We therefore expect that it will also push for a correction of competition-distorting regulations at the European level."

The leaders of the governing coalition of the Union and SPD have agreed to provide relief for the industry. For example, the air traffic tax is to be reduced to its pre-May 2024 level as of July 1, 2026. Additionally, there will be no further increase in air navigation charges in 2026; instead, a first reduction is planned. "Beyond that, we will ensure that fees decrease by well over ten percent in total by 2029," they stated. Furthermore, costs for airport security checks are to be reduced through better and more efficient procedures.

Germany continues to lag behind the recovery of European aviation since the coronavirus crisis. The reason is that many low-cost carriers such as Ryanair and EasyJet have reduced their flight offerings, citing high location costs in Germany as justification.

(Reporting by Klaus Lauer; Edited by Hans Busemann; For inquiries, please contact our editorial teams at berlin.newsroom@thomsonreuters.com (for politics and economy) or frankfurt.newsroom@thomsonreuters.com (for companies and markets).)

Intesa Sanpaolo Sees Surge in US Market Operations
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Intesa Sanpaolo, through its IMI Corporate & Investment Banking division led by Mauro Micillo, is reinforcing its role as a financial bridge with the United States, as reported by Milano Finanza on Friday.

In 2025, IMI CIB supported international issuers in Yankee bond transactions totaling over USD 23 billion, and assisted American companies in euro-denominated issuances - known as Reverse Yankee - for approximately USD 10 billion.

According to MF, these transactions amount to more than USD 30 billion overall, underscoring the strength of the division's cross-market model.

By Claudia Cavaliere, Alliance News reporter

Comments and questions: redazione@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2025 Alliance News IS Italian Service Ltd. All rights reserved.

BHP rolls out Australia's first battery-electric locomotives
data.description:
BHP Group Ltd - Melbourne, Australia-based diversified miner - Rolls out Australia's first brand new purpose-built battery-electric locomotives in the Port Hedland. The two BELs, developed with Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania-based Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies Corp, will be trialled on one of BHP's iron ore rail routes between its Pilbara mines and Port Hedland operations. Each locomotive houses a seven megawatt hour battery and uses regenerative braking to recapture energy on downhill runs. BHP says it supports the company's efforts to cut Scope 1 and 2 emissions across its iron ore rail network. Commissioning is underway ahead of track trials to test performance in live operations.

Tim Day, BHP Western Australia Iron Ore Asset president, says: "It marks the beginning of an important trial to understand how this technology can help us reduce diesel use, lower associated operational greenhouse gas emissions and improve efficiency across our rail network."

Current stock price: AUD42.75

12-month change: up 6.9%

By Judy Amaca, Alliance News reporter Asia-Pacific

Comments and questions to newsroom@alliancenews.com

Copyright 2025 Alliance News Ltd. All Rights Reserved.

Green Criticism and Aviation Praise for Black-Red Coalition Decisions
data.description:
BERLIN (dpa-AFX) - Criticism from the Greens and environmentalists, and praise from the aviation sector: The results of the coalition committee have sparked mixed reactions to the black-red government alliance. The announcement to reverse the air travel ticket tax increase, previously decided by the former traffic light coalition, has been particularly divisive. The leaders of the CDU, CSU, and SPD held discussions lasting around three and a half hours in the evening. Some of their plans had already been fundamentally announced in advance.

Who is voicing criticism

Green Party leader Felix Banaszak accused Chancellor Friedrich Merz (CDU) and the coalition leaders from CDU, CSU, and SPD of lacking courage. "Believing that the economic crisis can be solved by slashing the air travel tax and creating a 'Germany Fund' is quite presumptuous," he said.

Greenpeace called the reversal of the air travel tax increase a disastrous signal, referencing the ongoing world climate conference in Brazil: "While the international community at COP30 in Belém is struggling to reduce CO2 emissions, the German government is handing out tax and price breaks to fossil industries," German Greenpeace chief Martin Kaiser told Deutsche Presse-Agentur. He said this would further fuel global warming and undermine Germany's credibility.

Who is voicing praise

The aviation industry, on the other hand, predictably welcomed the planned reduction in ticket taxes. Their associations praised it as an important step to strengthen the sector and Germany as an aviation hub.

Ralph Beisel, CEO of the ADV airport association, called it "the beginning of a true trend reversal." His counterpart at the industry association BDL, Joachim Lang, added, "For Germany to benefit from the ongoing aviation boom in Europe, further steps will be needed in the coming years." Lufthansa stated that the government has shown it understands the seriousness of the situation in the aviation sector.

What the ticket tax is about

The coalition plans to lower the tax as of July 1, 2026. Merz mentioned a figure of about 350 million euros in favor of Germany's aviation industry. Additionally, measures will be taken to ensure that air traffic control fees do not rise further in 2026, but are instead reduced for the first time. The tax on departures from German airports was introduced in 2011 by the then black-yellow government to help balance the budget.

Currently, the tax per flight is 15.53 euros for domestic and short-haul flights, 39.34 euros for medium-haul, and 70.83 euros for long-haul destinations. The airlines are responsible for paying these amounts.

The industry has long called for a reduction in the air travel tax. International airlines are increasingly basing their aircraft at locations with lower entry costs, to the detriment of German airports. Domestic flights and direct routes from smaller and medium-sized airports to destinations abroad have been particularly affected in Germany.

What else was decided: Industrial electricity price

As of January 1, 2026, a state-subsidized, lower industrial electricity price will be introduced until 2028. Merz had already announced this at the "Steel Summit" a week ago. He mentioned a target price of 5 cents per kilowatt hour. This will apply to energy-intensive companies facing international competition, such as the steel and chemical industries.

Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil, who participated in the meeting as SPD chairman and vice chancellor, estimated costs at three to five billion euros, to be financed from the Climate and Transformation Fund, a special federal fund. Banaszak criticized this financing approach: "An industrial electricity price is the right move. But it has nothing to do with climate protection."

New gas-fired power plants

These plants are intended to serve as reserves in the future, stepping in when renewable energy sources cannot meet demand due to lack of sun or wind. To make investments worthwhile for energy companies, state support will be provided, pending approval from the EU Commission. The coalition expects the green light soon.

Merz said that next year, eight gigawatts should be put out to tender, with the plants to be operational by 2031. They should be technically capable of using hydrogen and, in line with climate goals, be fully decarbonized by 2045 at the latest, meaning they operate without carbon emissions.

Germany Fund

Under its umbrella, several financing instruments outlined in the coalition agreement will be linked to stimulate private investment. Klingbeil said a "docking point" should be created for private capital, for example in energy, resilience, or start-ups in security policy. Germany lags internationally in financing young, innovative companies./and/DP/mis
